sourceEvidence.label: Recurs Linux process boundary and pinned Codex/Bubblewrap references

assessment.summary: Linux tool children need one enforceable boundary between approved intent and host authority.

nonNegotiables: Preserve the TypeScript process and permission contracts.
Never silently downgrade a selected sandbox to ambient host execution.
Keep published security claims narrower than the implemented boundary.
assumptions: No measured process-start latency or memory budget was supplied.
A distribution Bubblewrap package is an acceptable Linux prerequisite.

opportunities.title: Remove ambient Linux authority from tool children
opportunities.summary: Enforce the existing workspace and network policy at an OS process boundary.
opportunities.diagnosis: The shared launcher cleans and bounds Linux children but currently executes them in host namespaces with ambient filesystem, process, IPC, and network authority.

evidence.claim: Workspace sandboxing is implemented only with macOS Seatbelt and rejects Linux.
The standalone Linux runtime defaults to local_guarded.
The CLI documentation discloses that Linux arbitrary commands retain host authority.
The current Codex Bubblewrap composition is a relevant implementation analogue, while its separate seccomp layer must not be implied by a namespace-only Recurs slice.

desiredInvariants: A workspace-sandboxed Linux child cannot modify the host outside the canonical workspace and its private process root.
A child cannot observe known host credential paths, runtime sockets, or host processes; other host files remain read-only.
Network access exists only when an approved intent includes the network category.
Sandbox unavailability is a terminal typed error and never triggers a downgrade.

options.title: Keep guarded host execution
options.summary: Retain environment filtering, approvals, and process bounds without an OS namespace boundary.

evidenceCoverage.rationale: Application guards do not remove ambient post-exec authority.
The namespace policy guidance is not applied.

tradeoffs.assessment: The current ambient Linux authority remains.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Run a command that writes outside the workspace and observes host sockets.

tradeoffs.assessment: No new launch work is introduced.
No new process or namespace metadata is introduced.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: No additional validation beyond existing process tests is required.
Run the full current Linux suite.

tradeoffs.assessment: No system package is required, but operators retain a major security limitation.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Keep the limitation visible in doctor and CLI documentation.

residualRisks: An approved arbitrary process retains the invoking user's host authority.

options.title: Use a trusted system Bubblewrap boundary
options.summary: Wrap the existing shared launch path in a fixed mount/user/PID/network namespace policy.

evidenceCoverage.rationale: The shared launcher, default, CI, and docs must all change together.
The namespace policy is applied, but Recurs does not yet add Codex's separate seccomp layer.

tradeoffs.assessment: Filesystem writes, host process visibility, runtime sockets, and unapproved network are removed from child authority; syscall exposure remains.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Run live Linux boundary tests for writes, credential/runtime visibility, process visibility, and network deny/allow.

tradeoffs.assessment: Each tool process adds one helper launch and namespace setup.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Record repeated no-op command startup time on Linux CI.

tradeoffs.assessment: Bubblewrap and namespace metadata add small transient memory use.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Sample peak RSS for repeated short commands if startup overhead becomes material.

tradeoffs.assessment: Commands fail closed when Bubblewrap or unprivileged user namespaces are unavailable.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Test missing/untrusted helper errors and run on supported CI distributions.

tradeoffs.assessment: Linux installs gain one explicit distribution package prerequisite.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Add CI installation and exact troubleshooting text.

tradeoffs.assessment: The stable profile and process APIs remain unchanged; no stored state migrates.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Run existing one-shot and streaming process tests unchanged.

residualRisks: No Recurs-owned seccomp syscall policy is installed.
Approved network commands retain access to the host network namespace.
Read-only system paths remain readable unless explicitly hidden.

workPackages: Add and validate the fixed Bubblewrap launch policy.
Switch the Linux default and add live tests.
Update CI, requirements, and exact security disclosures.
acceptanceCriteria: Host writes outside the workspace do not persist.
Host credential and runtime state are hidden while non-credential host files remain read-only.
Network deny and allow match approved intent.
Unavailability fails closed with a typed error.
migrationNotes: Install the Bubblewrap package before running Recurs on Linux.
implementationReadiness.rollback: Revert the launcher/default/test/doc changes; no data migration is required.

options.title: Bundle a Recurs native sandbox helper
options.summary: Own Bubblewrap composition, no_new_privs, and a reviewed seccomp policy in a shipped helper.

evidenceCoverage.rationale: The shared launcher and published defaults still require integration work.
A native helper can compose namespaces and syscall controls, subject to a separate policy review.

tradeoffs.assessment: A reviewed syscall policy can reduce the residual kernel interface beyond namespace containment.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Threat-model and adversarially test the exact helper and syscall allowlist.

tradeoffs.assessment: Namespace and filter installation add launch work similar to or above system Bubblewrap.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Benchmark the built helper against system Bubblewrap.

tradeoffs.assessment: The shipped helper and transient runtime add bounded but unmeasured cost.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Measure binary size and peak process-tree RSS.

tradeoffs.assessment: Owned diagnostics can improve failure clarity, while syscall compatibility can break legitimate tools.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Build a compiler, package-manager, MCP, and language-server compatibility matrix.

tradeoffs.assessment: Recurs must own multi-architecture builds, provenance, distribution, and updates.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Complete a binary release and supply-chain review before adoption.

tradeoffs.assessment: The process API can stay stable, but release packaging gains a native artifact on every supported target.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Exercise clean installs and upgrades on each supported distribution and architecture.

residualRisks: A permissive or incomplete syscall policy can overstate protection.
The helper supply chain becomes security-critical.

acceptanceCriteria: A separately reviewed threat model and binary provenance path exist.
migrationNotes: Adopt only after the system-Bubblewrap seam is stable.
implementationReadiness.rollback: Select the system-Bubblewrap backend without changing stored state.

opportunities.recommendation: Use a trusted system Bubblewrap boundary now; select a native helper later only when syscall-policy and binary-distribution ownership are justified.

openQuestions: Which Linux distributions and architectures define the first supported release matrix?
What compatibility evidence should trigger investment in a Recurs-owned seccomp helper?
